variants.title: Level 1: What is OOP? (Student Class)
variants.description: Complete the definition of a simple Student class.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use `String name;` and `int age;`

variants.title: Level 1: What is OOP? (Car Class)
variants.description: Complete the definition of a simple Car class.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use `String brand;` and `int year;`

variants.title: Level 1: What is OOP? (Book Class)
variants.description: Complete the definition of a simple Book class.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use String title; and String author;

variants.title: Level 2: Creating Objects (Student)
variants.description: Create an object from the Student class.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use `Student student1 = new Student();`

variants.title: Level 2: Creating Objects (Car)
variants.description: Create an object from the Car class.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use `Car myCar = new Car();`

variants.title: Level 3: Object Initialization (Student)
variants.description: Set values for the student object.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use `student1.name = "Alice";` and `student1.age = 20;`

variants.title: Level 3: Object Initialization (Car)
variants.description: Set values for the car object.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use `myCar.brand = "Toyota";` and `myCar.year = 2023;`

variants.title: Level 4: Multiple Objects (Students)
variants.description: Create and initialize multiple Student objects.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Create two Student objects and set their properties

variants.title: Level 4: Multiple Objects (Cars)
variants.description: Create and initialize multiple Car objects.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Create two Car objects and set their properties

variants.title: Level 5: Class Methods (Student)
variants.description: Add a method to display student information.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use `void displayInfo() { System.out.println(name + " is " + age + " years old"); }`

variants.title: Level 5: Class Methods (Car)
variants.description: Add a method to display car information.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use `void showDetails() { System.out.println(brand + " " + year); }`

variants.title: Level 1: Basic Object (Person)
variants.description: Complete the code to set obj1's age.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use `obj1.age = 20;`

variants.title: Level 1: Basic Object (Employee)
variants.description: Complete the code to set emp1's salary.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use `emp1.salary = 50000;`

variants.title: Level 2: Multiple Objects (Person)
variants.description: Create obj2 and set its name.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Follow how obj1 was created and add a name for obj2.

variants.title: Level 2: Multiple Objects (Employee)
variants.description: Create emp2 and set its department.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Follow how emp1 was created and add a department for emp2.

variants.title: Level 3: New Field (Person)
variants.description: Add an address field and assign it to obj1.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Add `String address;` in the class.

variants.title: Level 3: New Field (Employee)
variants.description: Add an id field and assign it to emp1.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Add `int id;` in the class.

variants.title: Level 4: Relationships (Person)
variants.description: Make obj1 reference obj2 as a friend.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Add `Person friend;` in the class, then connect obj1 to obj2.

variants.title: Level 4: Relationships (Employee)
variants.description: Make emp1 reference emp2 as a manager.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Add `Employee manager;` in the class, then connect emp1 to emp2.

variants.title: Level 5: Challenge (Person)
variants.description: Fill in details for two persons and connect them as friends.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Combine everything — fill obj1 and obj2, then connect them.

variants.title: Level 5: Challenge (Employee)
variants.description: Fill in details for two employees and connect them as manager-employee.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Fill both employees' details, then connect emp1 to emp2 as manager.

variants.title: Level 1: Basic CSS Selector (Paragraph)
variants.description: Style the paragraph text to be blue using CSS.
variants.skeleton: This text should be blue.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use `p { color: blue; }`

variants.title: Level 1: Basic CSS Selector (Heading)
variants.description: Style the heading text to be red using CSS.
variants.skeleton: This heading should be red.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use `h1 { color: red; }`

variants.title: Level 2: Multiple Selectors (H1 and P)
variants.description: Style both h1 and p elements with different colors.
variants.skeleton:
This heading is red

This paragraph should be green.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Add `p { color: green; }` rule.

variants.title: Level 2: Multiple Selectors (H2 and Span)
variants.description: Style both h2 and span elements with different colors.
variants.skeleton:
This heading is purple

This paragraph has orange text inside.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Add `span { color: orange; }` rule.

variants.title: Level 3: Class Selector (Highlight)
variants.description: Create a CSS class called 'highlight' that makes text yellow background.
variants.skeleton:
This text should have yellow background.

This text is normal.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use `.highlight { background-color: yellow; }`

variants.title: Level 3: Class Selector (Important)
variants.description: Create a CSS class called 'important' that makes text bold and red.
variants.skeleton:
This text should be red and bold.

This text is normal.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use `.important { color: red; font-weight: bold; }`

variants.title: Level 4: ID Selector (Main Title)
variants.description: Style the element with ID 'main-title' to be centered and large.
variants.skeleton:
Main Title

Regular paragraph text.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use `#main-title { font-size: 24px; text-align: center; }`

variants.title: Level 4: ID Selector (Header)
variants.description: Style the element with ID 'header' to have blue background and white text.
variants.skeleton:
This is the header

Regular paragraph text.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use `#header { background-color: blue; color: white; }`

variants.title: Level 5: Combined Styling Challenge (Card)
variants.description: Apply multiple CSS properties to create a styled card layout.
variants.skeleton:
Card Title

This is a card with styled content.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Add border, padding, background-color to .card and style .card h2

variants.title: Level 5: Combined Styling Challenge (Button)
variants.description: Create a styled button with hover effects.
variants.skeleton: Hover over the button to see the effect.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Style .btn with padding and background, then add .btn:hover with different background

variants.title: Level 1: Basic Inheritance (Animal → Dog)
variants.description: Create a Dog class that inherits from Animal.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Use `class Dog extends Animal {}`

variants.title: Level 1: Basic Inheritance (Vehicle → Car)
variants.description: Create a Car class that inherits from Vehicle.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Use `class Car extends Vehicle {}`

variants.title: Level 2: Add Child Properties (Dog)
variants.description: Add specific properties to the Dog class.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Add `String breed;` inside Dog class

variants.title: Level 2: Add Child Properties (Car)
variants.description: Add specific properties to the Car class.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Add `int doors;` inside Car class

variants.title: Level 3: Using Inherited Properties (Dog)
variants.description: Create a Dog object and set both inherited and own properties.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Use myDog.name, myDog.age, and myDog.breed to set values

variants.title: Level 3: Using Inherited Properties (Car)
variants.description: Create a Car object and set both inherited and own properties.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Use myCar.brand, myCar.year, and myCar.doors to set values

variants.title: Level 4: Method Inheritance (Animal → Dog)
variants.description: Add a method to Animal and use it in Dog.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Add `void makeSound() { System.out.println("Animal sound"); }` and call `myDog.makeSound();`

variants.title: Level 4: Method Inheritance (Vehicle → Car)
variants.description: Add a method to Vehicle and use it in Car.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Add `void start() { System.out.println("Vehicle started"); }` and call `myCar.start();`

variants.title: Level 5: Method Overriding (Dog)
variants.description: Override the makeSound method in Dog class.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Add `void makeSound() { System.out.println("Woof!"); }` in Dog class

variants.title: Level 5: Method Overriding (Car)
variants.description: Override the start method in Car class.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Add `void start() { System.out.println("Car engine started!"); }` in Car class

variants.title: Level 1: Private Fields (Person)
variants.description: Make the name field private in Person class.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Use `private String name;`

variants.title: Level 1: Private Fields (BankAccount)
variants.description: Make the balance field private in BankAccount class.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Use `private double balance;`

variants.title: Level 2: Getter Method (Person)
variants.description: Add a getter method for the private name field.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Add `String getName() { return name; }`

variants.title: Level 2: Getter Method (BankAccount)
variants.description: Add a getter method for the private balance field.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Add `double getBalance() { return balance; }`

variants.title: Level 3: Setter Method (Person)
variants.description: Add a setter method for the private name field.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Add `void setName(String newName) { name = newName; }`

variants.title: Level 3: Setter Method (BankAccount)
variants.description: Add a setter method for the private balance field.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Add `void setBalance(double newBalance) { balance = newBalance; }`

variants.title: Level 4: Using Getters and Setters (Person)
variants.description: Use getter and setter methods to access private fields.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Use `person.setName("John");` and `System.out.println(person.getName());`

variants.title: Level 4: Using Getters and Setters (BankAccount)
variants.description: Use getter and setter methods to access private balance.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Use `account.setBalance(1000.0);` and `System.out.println(account.getBalance());`

variants.title: Level 5: Validation in Setters (Person)
variants.description: Add validation in setter to ensure age is not negative.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Add `if (newAge >= 0) { age = newAge; }`

variants.title: Level 5: Validation in Setters (BankAccount)
variants.description: Add validation in setter to ensure balance is not negative.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Add `if (newBalance >= 0) { balance = newBalance; }`

variants.title: Level 1: Base Method (Animal)
variants.description: Create a base method in Animal class that can be overridden.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Add `void makeSound() { System.out.println("Some generic animal sound"); }`

variants.title: Level 1: Base Method (Shape)
variants.description: Create a base method in Shape class that can be overridden.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Add `void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing a generic shape"); }`

variants.title: Level 2: Override Method (Dog)
variants.description: Override the makeSound method in Dog class.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Add `void makeSound() { System.out.println("Woof! Woof!"); }`

variants.title: Level 2: Override Method (Circle)
variants.description: Override the draw method in Circle class.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Add `void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing a circle"); }`

variants.title: Level 3: Polymorphic Reference (Animal)
variants.description: Use Animal reference to point to Dog object.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Use `Animal animal = new Dog();` then `animal.makeSound();`

variants.title: Level 3: Polymorphic Reference (Shape)
variants.description: Use Shape reference to point to Circle object.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Use `Shape shape = new Circle();` then `shape.draw();`

variants.title: Level 4: Multiple Polymorphic Objects (Animals)
variants.description: Create array of Animal references with different animal types.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Create `Animal[] animals = {new Dog(), new Cat()};` and loop with `animals[i].makeSound();`

variants.title: Level 4: Multiple Polymorphic Objects (Shapes)
variants.description: Create array of Shape references with different shape types.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Create `Shape[] shapes = {new Circle(), new Rectangle()};` and loop with `shapes[i].draw();`

variants.title: Level 5: Method Overloading (Calculator)
variants.description: Create method overloading for add method with different parameters.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Create three add methods with different parameter types/counts

variants.title: Level 5: Abstract Polymorphism (Vehicle)
variants.description: Demonstrate runtime polymorphism with different vehicle types.
variants.hint: 🔧 Hint: Create `void testVehicle(Vehicle v) { v.start(); }` and call with Car and Motorcycle objects
